---
title: "The architecture: template, instance, wiki, fabric"
description: "The load-bearing distinction is three layers earlier drafts collapsed: tools install into the instance, but subtype and bundle-publishing are properties of the wiki, and the instance is the hub."
date: "2026-07-20"
categories: [research-agents, architecture]
draft: false
---

::: {.callout-note title="Status"}
**Accepted design.** The interaction diagram is at version 1.0; its core pieces run today.
:::

**Thesis.** The accepted interaction diagram separates three things earlier drafts collapsed into one.

The **template** is the shared, agent-neutral substrate: SCHEMA, verification gate, knowledge-graph extractor, ontology, installers. Every project reuses it. The **instance** is one instantiation of that template, a concrete project repo, the operational shell the user actually works in. The **llm-wiki** is the dedicated knowledge base created inside the instance, a separate git repo. It is the *wiki*, not the instance shell, that carries a subtype and is published as a bundle.

Why the separation matters, in one line: tools install into the instance; subtype and bundle-publishing are properties of the wiki. Collapsing them (as two earlier drafts did) hides where tools live and what actually gets shared.

The instance is the hub. The user and their LLM work in it. The template instantiates it and supplies built-in tools. Marketplaces install additional tools into it. The federation is a bidirectional connection, not a terminal: the instance's wiki is published as a bundle, and the instance `ask`s to consume other groups' bundles. Marketplaces are to tools what the federation is to bundles: distribution-and-discovery layers under one visibility ladder (public, private, institutional, commercial), where neither layer enforces visibility, the artifact does.

A second rendering, the public flyer's data-fabric styling, is a persuasion lens on the same system, and drawing it sharpened one thing: instantiation produces two coupled things sitting *on* the fabric, an agent and its llm-wiki memory, and that memory is reachable both by its own agent and via the federation. The fabric, not the template, is the substrate everything connects through.

Two earlier drafts were rejected along the way: one that put an abstract "consumer" at the top with pipelines flowing up to it, and one that drew the instance as a single box, hiding the wiki inside it.
